title: Blog 1
content:
SECURELY PROVIDING DATABASE CREDENTIALS TO LAMBDA FUNCTIONS USING AWS SECRETS MANAGER Using AWS Secrets Manager allows you to secure database credentials and pass them to AWS Lambda functions when connecting to Amazon RDS (MySQL). This solution eliminates the need to hardcode passwords in source code or pass them via environment variables, thereby protecting the backend database more securely. Additionally, the automatic periodic password rotation feature helps minimize security risks.
The operational model of the solution includes
The client sends a Request to a RESTful API hosted on AWS API Gateway. API Gateway executes the corresponding AWS Lambda function. The Lambda function calls the AWS Secrets Manager API to retrieve database login information (username/password). The Lambda function uses that information to connect, query the Amazon RDS (MySQL) database, and return the results. Deployment process via CloudFormation:
An AWS CloudFormation template is used to automate the provisioning of:
An RDS MySQL database (db.t3.micro instance type). Two Lambda functions: One (LambdaRDSCFNInit) to create an Employees table and insert sample data immediately after stack creation; another (LambdaRDSTest) to query the employee count. A RESTful API Gateway with a GET method. A Secret resource in Secrets Manager with a randomly generated password. Key technical points:
Dynamic References: CloudFormation uses dynamic references to retrieve the password from Secrets Manager when creating the RDS instance. This ensures CloudFormation does not log or store the password in plain text. Automatic Rotation: The AWS SecretsManager RotationSchedule resource is configured in coordination with a rotation Lambda function to automatically change the RDS database password every 30 days. Combining Lambda with AWS Secrets Manager helps automatically manage the lifecycle of sensitive information, reduces the operational cost of dedicated security infrastructure, and significantly enhances the security of Serverless applications.

title: Event 1

FCAJ Community Day – Effective Learning & AI Prompt Engineering Event Content The Community Day featured two main sharing sessions from senior members of the FCAJ community:
Session 1 – Effective Learning Strategies (Speaker: A Long) A Long shared practical tips for building and maintaining an effective learning routine:
Create a learning chain: Set up a consistent daily learning streak and commit to keeping it unbroken. Maintain focus: Actively avoid distractions such as games and social media during study periods. Reward system: Give yourself small rewards after completing a streak or finishing a study session to reinforce the habit. Session 2 – AI Prompt Engineering & Proptimizer Demo (Speaker: A Thịnh) A Thịnh presented a demo of Proptimizer — a browser extension that optimizes prompts and lets users chat with AI anywhere on the web. The session covered the following topics:
What Goes Into a Great Prompt? Component Description Role Who the model is pretending to be Instruction What the model should do Context Relevant background information Input Data Text to be analyzed / acted upon Output Format Structure, syntax, style Examples Few-shot demonstrations Constraints Guidelines such as “do NOT…”, focus areas, length limits Prompt Example [Role] Career coach for fresh graduates [Task] Improve my internship self-introduction [Context] DevOps Engineer with AWS, backend, and project experience [Input] "Hello, my name is Thinh..." [Example] "I like programming and teamwork." → "I enjoy programming and work well with others to solve problems." [Output] Improved version + short explanation [Constraints] Under 80 words, simple, natural, clear, confident Mastering LLM Prompting – Best Practices Be Clear & Specific Use Directive Language Use Delimiters to Separate Sections Describe DOs, not DON’Ts Don’t Ask LLMs to Do Math Allow “I Don’t Know” Responses Break Long Inputs into Smaller Segments Token Economics: Understanding AI Costs Tokens = Subword units (not always full words) LLMs read and generate text in tokens Token counts differ across languages Model Example – Claude Opus 4.6: Input tokens: $5.00 / 1M tokens Output tokens: $25.00 / 1M tokens Advanced Prompting Techniques Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting — step-by-step reasoning Self-Consistency — multiple reasoning paths, pick the most consistent Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) — branching exploration of ideas Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — combining retrieval with generation Role Prompting — assigning a persona to the model Proptimizer Architecture The extension is built on a fully serverless AWS stack:
Amazon Cognito for user authentication (Sign up / Sign in) CloudFront + S3 for static content delivery API Gateway + Lambda for backend logic, secured by a Cognito Authorizer Amazon Bedrock for LLM inference DynamoDB for data persistence CloudWatch for logging and metrics I apologize for only noting Mr. Long and Mr. Thinh, but forgetting to note the others later in the event.
Lessons Learned & Personal Takeaways Learning discipline matters: Maintaining a consistent learning streak and reducing distractions are critical to long-term knowledge retention. Prompt engineering is a skill: Understanding prompt structure (Role, Task, Context, Constraints) dramatically improves the quality of AI-generated outputs. Cost awareness: Knowing how tokens work and how much they cost helps make cost-effective decisions when building AI-powered products. Serverless AI architecture: The Proptimizer demo showcased a practical, production-grade serverless architecture using Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, Bedrock, and DynamoDB — a pattern directly applicable to our SaaS HR project. title: Overview & Architecture
content:
1. Idea & Objectives Context & Problem Small and medium businesses (SMEs) need an HR system (employees, departments, payroll data) but do not want to run and maintain their own servers. A single shared SaaS must keep each company’s data strictly separated.
Target Customer SME companies that subscribe to the SaaS to manage HR. Each company = one isolated tenant (data isolation enforced per request via tenant_id).
Goals Deliver a multi-tenant HR SaaS on AWS with tenant data isolation. Highly available (Multi-AZ) and horizontally scalable application tier. Managed, low-ops services (no self-managed servers). Success Criteria End-to-end login → authenticated API → data read/write works. Async event (tenant → HR) delivered via SQS. Survives an AZ / RDS failure (Multi-AZ failover). Monthly cost kept ~$130 (24/7), near $0 when torn down. Fit with the program Real-world AWS use-case using ≥3 managed services — covers compute, data, identity, messaging, edge, and monitoring.
2. Architecture Description Architecture Diagram Three tiers inside one VPC across two Availability Zones:
Presentation: CloudFront (edge) → S3 (private static, OAC). Application: ALB (public) → ECS Fargate (private) running auth / tenant / hr. Data: RDS MySQL Multi-AZ (private). Async via SQS; auth via app-signed RS256 JWT. AWS Services Used Layer Service Role Edge CloudFront CDN + single entry (static + API) Static S3 (private + OAC) React build artifacts Identity App-signed RS256 JWT auth-service signs JWT (\tenant_id + role claims); tenant/hr verify via public key. Cognito pool provisioned, not wired in. Load balancing Application Load Balancer Path-routing /api/v1/* to ECS Compute ECS Fargate (3 services) auth / tenant / hr microservices Registry ECR Container images Database RDS MySQL db.t4g.micro Multi-AZ Primary + Standby, 3 logical DBs Async SQS (+ DLQ) tenant → queue → hr Secrets SSM Parameter Store DB password, SQS URL, JWT keys, Cognito IDs Monitoring CloudWatch + SNS Logs + CPU alarm → email Network VPC, IGW, 1 NAT Gateway 6 subnets / 2 AZ Why these services (rationale) Service Why chosen ECS Fargate Serverless containers — no EC2 to patch/scale-manage; native auto scaling. RDS Multi-AZ Managed HA database with synchronous standby + automatic failover + backups. SQS Decouples tenant→hr, absorbs spikes, survives consumer outage; DLQ for poison messages; no broker to run. App-signed RS256 JWT auth-service = single issuer (private key); tenant/hr = stateless verifiers (public key). No shared secret, no external IdP. (Cognito pool provisioned but not in the flow.) CloudFront + S3 Cheap, edge-cached static hosting; S3 stays private via OAC. ALB L7 path-routing to 3 services + health checks. SSM Parameter Store Free SecureString secrets — no hard-coded keys in images.

title: Proposal
content:
SaaS HR Multi-Tenant Microservices A Unified AWS Cloud Solution for Enterprise-Scale Multi-Tenant HR Management 1. Executive Summary SaaS HR Multi-Tenant Microservices is an enterprise-scale multi-tenant Human Resource management (SaaS) platform designed to enhance HR administration efficiency and optimize operational costs for organizations. The system supports up to thousands of independent businesses (tenants) operating on the same platform thanks to a strict logical data separation model (Pool Model). The solution leverages AWS Cloud services (ECS Fargate, RDS MySQL, SQS, Cognito) combined with asymmetric authentication via Nginx API Gateway to provide a secure, safe, and cost-effective real-time experience for system health monitoring, employee management, timekeeping, and leave management.
2. Problem Statement Current Problem Traditional HR management systems are often deployed individually for each company, leading to wasted server resources and high maintenance effort. For multi-tenant solutions, the primary challenge is ensuring absolute data isolation between client enterprises (preventing cross-tenant data leakage) while maintaining high performance during traffic spikes, and effectively controlling infrastructure costs.
The Solution The project develops a SaaS HR Multi-Tenant system based on a Microservices architecture decomposed into 3 core services: auth-service (authentication), tenant-service (tenant management), and hr-service (HR operations). The system implements logical data isolation (Pool Model) across 3 independent RDS MySQL databases (auth_db, tenant_db, hr_db). Using Amazon Cognito as a centralized identity provider integrated with RS256 JWT signing, it enables services to perform stateless token self-verification extremely fast. The ReactJS (Vite) frontend is hosted on S3 and distributed globally via CloudFront CDN. Asynchronous events between services are decoupled through Amazon SQS to prevent bottlenecking.
Benefits and Return on Investment (ROI)
Infrastructure Cost Optimization: By combining the 3 microservices databases into a single RDS MySQL Multi-AZ instance and leveraging serverless ECS Fargate, the estimated AWS infrastructure operational cost is optimized to around $115 - $155 USD/month (running 24/7). All infrastructure can be torn down (destroyed) when not in use to reduce costs to near $0 USD. High Availability & Auto Backup: Multi-AZ deployment minimizes downtime, provides auto-recovery, and supports automatic 7-day data backup retention. Reduced Deployment Time: New tenant clients can register and use the system immediately via a dedicated subdomain without any physical server installation or configuration steps. The estimated break-even period for the system is 3–6 months, driven by optimized employee productivity and reduced IT operational costs. 3. Solution Architecture The platform adopts a 3-tier architecture combined with Microservices on AWS to manage and operate the application. All compute resources are deployed within a VPC spanning 2 Availability Zones (AZs) to enhance fault tolerance.
AWS Services Used
Amazon CloudFront: CDN distributing the static frontend and acting as the single point of entry for the entire system. Amazon S3: Stores the statically built source code of the ReactJS application (securely accessed via OAC). Application Load Balancer (ALB): Receives traffic from CloudFront to route /api/v1/* requests into the ECS Fargate cluster. Amazon ECS Fargate: Runs stateless microservices containers (auth, tenant, hr) across both AZs. Amazon RDS MySQL: Multi-AZ Database Management System (Primary & Standby) running in private subnets, completely isolated from the internet. Amazon SQS: Manages message queues for asynchronous communication between tenant-service and hr-service. Amazon Cognito: User Pools managing user accounts and issuing JWTs containing tenant ID claims. AWS NAT Gateway, Route 53, ACM, CloudWatch, SNS, SSM Parameter Store. Component Design
Client Interface (Frontend): ReactJS (Vite) handles client-side routing, automatically detects the tenant via subdomain, and stores authentication tokens. API Gateway & Load Balancing: ALB dynamically routes to the corresponding target groups in ECS. Security Groups are configured following a Zero-Trust model, allowing only the ALB to connect to ECS Fargate, and ECS Fargate to connect to RDS MySQL. Service & Business Logic Layer: FastAPI microservices process logic independently. JWT tokens are verified statelessly at each service using the public key retrieved from Cognito, eliminating the need to call the authentication service. Database & Isolation Layer: Data is stored centrally in RDS MySQL. The employees and departments tables enforce logical isolation through a mandatory tenant_id field, optimized with indexes to accelerate queries and guarantee data isolation. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases The project is implemented in parallel between code development and Cloud infrastructure setup through 4 key phases:
Network Infrastructure & Security Design: Establish VPC, partitioning 6 isolated subnets for 3 tiers (Public, App Private, Data Private). Configure Route Tables and Security Groups. Local Source Code Development: Program the 3 FastAPI microservices, build the ReactJS interface, configure Nginx as a local API Gateway, and integrate the RS256 JWT mechanism. Integration & Data Security Verification: Perform testing of data isolation between tenants, and integrate the asynchronous message queuing system via Redis/SQS. Cloud Deployment & Optimization: Package Docker containers (multi-stage build), push to ECR, configure and launch services on AWS ECS Fargate, ALB, RDS Multi-AZ, and Cognito, and configure the CloudWatch Logs monitoring system. Technical Requirements
Local Development Workstation: Docker Desktop installed to run MySQL, Nginx Gateway, and 3 microservices in sync. Python 3.10+ for service APIs, and Node.js v18+ for the React frontend. AWS Cloud Platform: Requires an active AWS account with full permissions to provision VPC, ECS Fargate, RDS MySQL, Cognito User Pools, SQS, CloudFront, and S3. Proficient knowledge of optimized Docker packaging and asymmetric key verification mechanisms (RS256) is required. 5. Timeline & Milestones Sprint 1: Infrastructure Initialization & API Gateway Setup (Week 1): Project directory structuring, running sample database migrations (Alembic). Configure local Nginx gateway and React Vite scaffolding for login/dashboard. Sprint 2: Core Microservices & DB Association (Week 2): Build complete auth-service (RS256 authentication), tenant-service (subscription package management), and hr-service with core data schemas supporting logical isolation via tenant_id. Sprint 3: System Integration & Subdomain Routing (Week 3): Connect ReactJS frontend with API Gateway, configure Axios interceptors to auto-attach tokens, handle user authorization, and design intuitive HR management interfaces. Sprint 4: Container Packaging, Security & Cloud Deployment (Week 4): Light production Docker packaging (multi-stage), security scanning (Trivy), provisioning AWS infrastructure, and conducting final system verification tests with a single command. 6. Budget Estimation Operational cost estimation on AWS (Singapore Region, running 24/7):
AWS Service Proposed Configuration Estimated / Month NAT Gateway 1 shared NAT Gateway in the public subnet $43 – $50 RDS MySQL Multi-AZ db.t4g.micro instance x2, 20 GB gp3 storage & backups $30 – $38 Application Load Balancer 1 ALB for load balancing + ~1 LCU $22 – $26 ECS Fargate 4–6 tasks running services (0.25 vCPU / 0.5 GB) $12 – $18 Data Transfer / CloudWatch Logs retained 7 days, NAT data transfer, inter-AZ $5 – $12 Route 53 + CloudFront + S3 + ECR Domain, CDN, React static hosting, Container registry $3 – $8 Cognito / SQS / SNS / ACM Covered under Free Tier limits $0 Total / Month ≈ $115 – $155 (~$130) [!NOTE] To optimize study and testing costs, all resources can be created and cleaned up (destroyed) quickly using CDK/Terraform scripts when not running a demo.
7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix
Cross-tenant Data Leakage: Impact: Critical; Probability: Low. API Gateway or Microservice Overload: Impact: High; Probability: Medium. AWS Budget Overrun: Impact: Medium; Probability: Medium. Mitigation Strategies
Data Leak Prevention: Establish verification and auto-injection of tenant_id conditions (extracted from verified JWT token claims) into all database query statements using FastAPI Dependency Injection. Overload Prevention: Configure Auto Scaling for ECS Fargate based on CPU/Memory usage, and decouple intensive processing workloads asynchronously using SQS queues. Cost Control: Activate AWS Budgets to alert via email (SNS) when projected or actual costs exceed $100 USD or $130 USD. Contingency Plans
Use automated cleanup tools/scripts for unused AWS resources (orphaned volumes, unused NAT gateways, stale Elastic IPs). Store RDS backup copies in compressed, secure, and isolated locations to recover from disaster scenarios. 8. Expected Outcomes Technical Improvements: A smoothly running multi-tenant system with absolute data isolation between clients, automated subdomain routing, and enterprise-grade secure authentication (RS256 JWT). Long-term Value: Establish high-quality reference design documentation and microservices architecture source code that is easily reusable for future SaaS projects.

title: Blog 2
content:
PROACTIVE INFRASTRUCTURE MONITORING SOLUTION USING AMBIENT AGENTS ON AMAZON BEDROCK In large-scale cloud infrastructure management (Multi-Account/Multi-Region), handling a massive volume of alerts (Alert Fatigue) from Amazon CloudWatch often consumes a significant amount of the operations team’s time. Traditional rule-based monitoring systems sometimes only detect issues after they have occurred, making it difficult to correlate log and metric data for Root Cause Analysis.
The AgentWatch solution provides a continuously operating Ambient Agent model, assisting engineering teams in gathering infrastructure data, analyzing system behavior, and providing Actionable Insights to optimize operational workflows.
Operational Architecture & Human-in-the-Loop Coordination:
AgentWatch is designed to operate alongside humans by combining automation with a strict approval process (Human-in-the-Loop - HITL), consisting of two main processing flows:
Scheduled Ambient Monitoring: Triggered periodically every 15 minutes by Amazon EventBridge. A LangChain Agent task integrating the Claude model on Amazon Bedrock uses dedicated APIs to query CloudWatch metrics, logs, and alarms from multiple AWS accounts. The LLM then processes the data, extracts key information, and sends a report to the team’s Slack channel. On-Demand Interaction & HITL Control: Engineers can directly query the Agent in real-time via Slack Slash Commands (e.g., /ask Analyze log patterns for the last hour). The system categorizes tasks based on risk levels: tasks involving reading information or trend analysis are processed automatically; meanwhile, sensitive infrastructure configuration changes strictly require a 3-step control process: Notify, Question (Clarification), and Review (Pending human approval). Core AWS Services in the Architecture:
The solution builds an automated workflow (Agentic Workflow) based on the coordination of Cloud-Native services:
Amazon Bedrock & Bedrock AgentCore Runtime: The platform providing the Foundation Model and a secure serverless runtime environment to operate AI Agents at an enterprise scale. Amazon EventBridge & AWS Lambda: Acts as the scheduler and orchestrator for the Agent’s workflow. Amazon CloudWatch & AWS STS: Provides centralized telemetry data and the assume role mechanism for Cross-Account Monitoring. Amazon API Gateway & Amazon Cognito: Ensures access control and OAuth 2.0 security for endpoints receiving webhooks from Slack. Practical Application Benefits:
Anomaly Detection Support: The LLM’s Natural Language Understanding (NLU) capabilities help link discrete signals from system logs and metrics, aiding in the detection of anomalies that fixed rule-based configurations might miss. MTTR Reduction Support: The Agent automatically performs initial context gathering and analysis steps as soon as an alert is detected, providing foundational information to help engineers shorten diagnostic time. Compliance and Governance Assurance: Reduces repetitive manual tasks for operational engineers while maintaining human control over the Production environment through clear approval steps. Conclusion: The AgentWatch solution demonstrates the potential of applying Generative AI (Agentic architecture) in optimizing Cloud Operations, shifting monitoring systems from passive information display to more proactive data analysis support.

title: Event 2
content:
FCAJ Community Day – AI Context Engineering, CloudFront & Multi-Agent Systems Event Content The second Community Day featured seven sharing sessions covering market trends, AI prompt/context engineering, AWS product demos, CDN architecture, hackathon experiences, LLM internals, and enterprise multi-agent systems.
Session 1 – Market Trends & Personal Value (Speaker: Anh Hưng) Anh Hưng shared insights on current and future market demands:
Understanding the evolving needs of the tech industry today and in the coming years. The importance of building your own personal value — developing unique skills and expertise that set you apart in a competitive market. Session 2 – Context Engineering for AI Prompts (Speaker: Anh Tịnh) Anh Tịnh presented best practices for writing effective AI context to maximize productivity:
Give AI the context it needs — be precise about what you want, don’t add redundant information the model already knows. Common mistake #1: Pulling everything from the internet and dumping it into context — this dilutes quality. Common mistake #2: Telling the AI things it already knows. Don’t over-explain — state the problem to be solved next. Shared a framework for writing standardized, effective context. Introduced Obsidian as a “Second AI Brain” — using Obsidian as a personal knowledge management system to feed structured context to AI tools. Session 3 – Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Q (AWS Demo) A demonstration of AWS’s platform connectivity ecosystem enabling users to self-build AI products:
AWS creates a platform connection across ecosystems so users can build their own products. Demo 1: From a single Excel file, the AI analyzes insights and creates an agent based on user needs. Demo 2: An agent that summarizes meeting content and automatically sends meeting notes to all participants via email. Session 4 – CloudFront as Your Foundation A deep-dive into Amazon CloudFront’s new capabilities and pricing model:
New Pricing Model Target user groups: Small website owners, business users, scaling businesses Prediction-based cost estimation Easy plan selection and switching Fixed-price CDN + security pricing plans Cost savings strategies with CloudFront and reduced utilization Security Capabilities HTTPS support with encryption Mutual TLS support for client certificate authentication Origin Cloaking with VPC Origin — creating a tunnel from CloudFront directly into the origin Origin Cloaking for Custom Origins — hiding origin servers from direct access Viewer Access Control — block traffic according to geographic area Content Protection with Signed URLs — prevent URL copying and sharing Built-in Failover and origin failover for high availability Performance Architecture Multi-layer caching architecture — aggregates traffic from CloudFront edge locations HTTP/3 (QUIC/UDP) multiplexing for faster connections Persistent connections to origins — no repeated TCP handshakes, maintaining TCP traffic efficiently CloudFront Functions — redirect users to region-specific endpoints Session 5 – Build a Product in 36 Hours with LotusHacks A speaker shared their hackathon experience at LotusHacks:
Built an app that allows direct interaction with UI components for live editing through 3 AI agents. The app provides edit history review, allowing users to see and revert past changes. Shared lessons learned from the intense 36-hour product-building sprint. Session 6 – Non-determinism of Deterministic LLM Settings A technical session on how LLMs work internally:
How LLMs generate text: The model ranks scores for each word in the prompt to select the most suitable next token from its vocabulary. Inference optimization methods: Current LLM providers use inference optimizations to deliver cost-effective pricing plans while maintaining output quality. Session 7 – Enterprise-grade Multi-Agent System: Startup Credit Scoring A case study on building a multi-agent system for evaluating startup creditworthiness:
The Problem Evaluating credit scores for startup businesses with limited historical data. All existing startup data cannot be fed into current models — the challenge is generating reliable AI outputs within a fixed budget. Every tool used and every line of code generated must deliver measurable value. Agent Architecture The system uses a Manager agent coordinating specialized sub-agents:
Financial Analyst — evaluate financial health Market Analyst — assess market positioning Team Evaluator — review founding team capabilities Risk Assessor — identify potential risks Compliance — ensure regulatory adherence Why Multi-Agent Architecture Works Specialized expertise — each agent focuses on its domain Checks & balances — agents cross-validate each other Parallel processing — multiple evaluations run simultaneously Auditability — each agent’s reasoning is traceable Scalability — add new agents as needs grow Fault tolerance — system remains functional even if one agent fails Key Principle Build systems that are safe, reliable, and user-supportive.
Lessons Learned & Personal Takeaways Personal branding matters: In a rapidly evolving market, building and showcasing your own unique value is essential for career growth. Context engineering > prompt engineering: The quality of AI output depends heavily on providing focused, relevant context — not just good prompts. Avoid over-explaining or flooding with irrelevant data. Obsidian as a knowledge system: Using structured personal knowledge bases like Obsidian can dramatically improve the context you provide to AI tools. CloudFront is more than a CDN: The new pricing model, VPC Origin cloaking, signed URLs, and HTTP/3 support make CloudFront a comprehensive edge security and performance platform. Multi-agent systems for complex problems: Decomposing a complex problem (like credit scoring) into specialized agents with checks and balances produces more reliable, auditable results than a single monolithic AI approach. LLM internals awareness: Understanding how token scoring and inference optimization work helps make better decisions when choosing providers and pricing plans. Event Photos

Configured detailed access controls for AWS resources using IAM Groups, Users, and Customer Managed Policies. Implemented secure temporary credentials and delegated access using IAM Roles rather than permanent security keys. Applied the principle of least privilege across administrative and user-level permissions. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Always use IAM Roles for temporary permissions and set session timeouts to limit exposure. Monitor identity access and API activities continuously using AWS CloudTrail. Secure Application Access (IAM Roles on EC2)
Replaced hardcoded IAM Access Keys and Secret Access Keys in application code with temporary credentials from an IAM Role. Provisioned an EC2 instance and S3 bucket to test secure, credential-less programmatic uploads. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Hardcoded credentials pose a high security risk. Always assign IAM Roles to EC2 instances for secure S3/AWS service access. Static Website Hosting (Amazon S3 & CloudFront)
Configured Amazon S3 static website hosting, utilizing S3 Standard storage with 99.999999999% durability. Integrated S3 hosting with Amazon CloudFront using Origin Access Control (OAC) to secure the origin bucket. Enabled S3 Versioning to preserve historical object states and configured Cross-Region Replication (CRR) from Singapore for geographic redundancy. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Keep S3 Block Public Access enabled and route traffic exclusively through CloudFront to enforce HTTPS and caching. Use S3 Lifecycle Policies to automatically transition or expire old object versions to control storage costs. Network Infrastructure (Amazon VPC)
Designed and provisioned custom VPCs with public subnets (routed to IGW) and private subnets (routed to NAT Gateway) across multiple AZs. Configured stateful Security Groups (instance-level) and stateless Network ACLs (subnet-level) for multi-layered network security. Configured Site-to-Site VPN with dual redundant IPSec tunnels and enabled VPC Flow Logs for traffic analysis. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: AWS reserves 5 IP addresses in each subnet (first 4 and last 1) for internal networking. Use NAT Gateways instead of NAT Instances in production for automatic scaling and lower administrative overhead. Compute Services (Amazon EC2)
Provisioned and connected to Windows Server 2025 (via RDP) and Amazon Linux 2023 (via SSH/Session Manager). Deployed a Node.js CRUD User Management application under LAMP (Linux) and XAMPP (Windows) environments. Created EBS snapshots, custom AMIs, and applied IAM governance policies to restrict EC2 regions, instance types, and volume categories. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Terminate unused instances and release associated Elastic IPs to prevent unexpected charges. Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for secure instance access without opening inbound SSH/RDP ports. Managed Databases (Amazon RDS)
Selected managed database engines (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Aurora) in Multi-AZ DB Subnet Groups. Configured Multi-AZ synchronous replication with automatic failover (60-120 seconds) and asynchronous Read Replicas for read-heavy workloads. Established backup management using automated point-in-time recovery (up to 35 days) and manual DB snapshots. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Automated snapshots are performed on standby Multi-AZ instances to prevent primary database latency. To encrypt an existing unencrypted database, copy its snapshot, apply KMS encryption to the copy, and restore from it. Compute Cost Optimization (Amazon Lightsail)
Provisioned simplified, low-cost application stacks (WordPress, Akaunting) and high-availability database instances on Lightsail. Minimized host attack surface by removing port 22 (SSH) from both IPv4 and IPv6 instance firewalls. Configured manual snapshots, automatic daily snapshots, and resource alarms (e.g. CPU utilization). 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Manual snapshots are persistent and not automatically deleted when the parent instance is terminated; clean them up manually to avoid storage costs. Application Autoscaling (EC2 Auto Scaling & ELB)
Created custom AMIs and configured Launch Templates to boot uniform application servers across multiple AZs. Provisioned Application Load Balancers (ALBs) and Target Groups, integrating them with Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). Evaluated scaling strategies (Manual, Scheduled, Dynamic, Predictive) and validated dynamic scaling by load testing with Paessler WebStress. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Integrate ELB with AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to offload SSL/TLS encryption (SSL termination) from target servers. Combine predictive scaling with target tracking dynamic scaling to proactively handle cyclical traffic spikes. Observability (Amazon CloudWatch)
Collected performance metrics and centralized application/system logs under CloudWatch Log Groups. Created custom metric alarms to trigger automated notifications and instance recovery actions. Built unified CloudWatch Dashboards to monitor overall application and infrastructure health. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Enable Container Insights to automatically collect and aggregate compute metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network) for ECS, EKS, and Fargate. Hybrid DNS Integration (Amazon Route 53)
Explored Route 53 Resolver capabilities including private hosted zones (PHZ) and recursive query resolution. Configured Inbound Endpoints (resolving AWS domains from local) and Outbound Endpoints (resolving local domains from AWS). Created Resolver Rules to conditionally forward queries for specific domain namespaces to local DNS servers. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Distribute DNS endpoints across multiple Availability Zones to prevent single points of failure in DNS routing. Command Line Administration (AWS CLI)
Configured the AWS CLI with default/named profiles on remote EC2 instances. Executed API operations and parsed text-based output using Unix utilities (grep, awk, sed). 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Use the ’text’ output format in shell scripts for easy integration and processing with command-line utilities. Always use IAM Roles instead of static credentials (access keys) to grant secure access.

Blog 3 - VPC LINK IN PRIVATE INTEGRATIONS OF AMAZON API GATEWAY This blog delves into the core technologies behind VPC Link in Amazon API Gateway (such as AWS Hyperplane and AWS PrivateLink) and compares the fundamental architectural differences between VPC Link for REST APIs and HTTP APIs.

title: Blog 3
content:
VPC LINK IN PRIVATE INTEGRATIONS OF AMAZON API GATEWAY VPC link is a resource in Amazon API Gateway that allows connecting API routes to internal, private resources located within a VPC network. The article delves into the core technologies behind VPC Link (such as AWS Hyperplane and AWS PrivateLink) and compares the fundamental architectural differences between VPC Link for REST APIs and HTTP APIs.
Core foundational technologies:
AWS Hyperplane: AWS’s internal network virtualization platform that supports connection and routing between different VPCs. Hyperplane uses VPC-to-VPC NAT instead of PrivateLink. AWS PrivateLink: Technology that allows accessing AWS services privately within the AWS internal network without routing through the public Internet. Traffic is routed through Interface VPC Endpoints (consumer side) and VPC Endpoint Services (provider side). Distinguishing Private API and Private Integration:
Private API: Means the API Gateway Endpoint can only be accessed from within a VPC (or via Direct Connect/VPN). Currently, only REST APIs support configuring Private APIs. Private Integration: Means the backend system (behind the API Gateway) is located within a VPC and is not publicly exposed to the Internet. API Gateway uses VPC Link to connect to this backend securely. Comparing VPC Link for REST API vs. HTTP API:
Underlying technology: The first difference lies in the underlying technology. VPC Link for REST APIs is based on AWS PrivateLink, while HTTP APIs use the VPC-to-VPC NAT network address translation system of AWS Hyperplane. This difference leads to major architectural changes underneath. Resources and integration capabilities: HTTP APIs are significantly more flexible as they do not require a VPC Endpoint Service and can connect directly to ALBs, NLBs, or services via AWS Cloud Map; a single VPC Link can also associate with multiple backends. Conversely, REST APIs strictly require a VPC Endpoint Service, connect through a single NLB, and configuring multi-backends is much more complex. Features and IP addresses: HTTP APIs leverage the Layer 7 power of ALBs (like advanced routing, authentication) and operate via an ENI tunneling mechanism. Meanwhile, REST APIs are more restricted at Layer 7 since they route through an NLB (Layer 4), and the backend system will identify the source IP as the private IP of the NLB itself. Understanding the differences between the two types of VPC Links helps architectural engineers make accurate decisions when designing systems. If you need a more cost-effective, faster API solution with flexible integration with ALBs or containers, VPC Link for HTTP APIs is the optimal choice. If you require the distinct, in-depth features of REST APIs, you will need to configure VPC Link via the PrivateLink mechanism with an NLB.
Post Link: AWS Study Group Facebook Post Blog Link: Understanding VPC links in Amazon API Gateway private integrations

title: Event 3
content:
FCAJ Community Day – Game Dev on AWS, Docker, GraphRAG & Career Growth Event Content The third Community Day featured five sharing sessions covering real-time game development on AWS, Docker containerization, GraphRAG for LLMs, AWS WAF with machine learning, and a career journey from IT helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin.
Session 1 – Rock-Paper-Scissors Multiplayer Game on AWS (Speaker: Bạn Bảo) Bạn Bảo demonstrated a real-time multiplayer Rock-Paper-Scissors game built with the Godot engine using WebSocket connections on AWS infrastructure:
Architecture AWS Lambda Functions for serverless backend logic Amazon DynamoDB for state management WebSocket API for real-time bidirectional communication Game State Management on DynamoDB The game handles multiple player states stored in DynamoDB:
Connected — player is online Disconnected — player has left Finding Match — player is looking for an opponent Choice — player has made their move How It Works The Godot client processes user input and sends requests to the server via WebSocket. The Lambda backend handles matchmaking, game logic, and result determination. Demo: Two clients connected simultaneously, and DynamoDB displayed both player IDs with a matched status. 3 Key Lessons Learned DynamoDB Scan is expensive at scale — scanning with 1,000+ concurrent players doesn’t guarantee speed; traffic and costs increase significantly. Lambda is stateless — each invocation is independent, so game state must be persisted externally (DynamoDB). Handle unexpected disconnects gracefully — players may disconnect mid-game; the system must detect and clean up orphaned sessions. Recommendation Mentioned AWS GameLift as a managed service purpose-built for multiplayer game server hosting at scale. Session 2 – Docker: A Containerization Technology (Speaker: Bạn Bảo) Bạn Bảo introduced Docker and containerization concepts:
Virtual Machines (VMs): What they are and their benefits — full OS isolation but heavy resource usage. Containers & Docker: “Build once, run anywhere.” Containers share the host OS kernel, making them lightweight. Efficiency example: Run 10 containers on 5 EC2 instances instead of needing 10 separate EC2 instances. Demo: Built and ran a basic application using docker build deployed across 2 EC2 machines. Session 3 – GraphRAG: Graph-enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Speaker: Bạn Phát) Bạn Phát presented GraphRAG as an evolution beyond traditional RAG:
What is RAG? Retrieval-Augmented Generation — fetching relevant documents to provide context for LLM responses. Limitations that make traditional RAG fall short: lack of relationship awareness between entities, difficulty with multi-hop reasoning. Why GraphRAG? GraphRAG understands relationship awareness between entities, providing more coherent and understandable context for LLMs. Captures connections between concepts that flat document retrieval misses. Implementation Approaches Approach Description Fully Managed Amazon Bedrock as the engine managing everything; Amazon Neptune stores and manages all the graph data Bedrock generates Custom Route Bedrock serves as the interface to call models; Foundation Models (FM) are handled by AWS; custom processing pipelines are built separately Session 4 – AWS WAF + Machine Learning for Network Intrusion Detection (Speaker: Bạn Đại) Bạn Đại presented a hybrid approach combining AWS WAF with ML for attack detection:
AWS WAF limitations: Rule-based detection alone cannot catch all sophisticated attack patterns. Solution: Combine WAF with Machine Learning models to detect network intrusions (NIDS — Network Intrusion Detection System). Data preprocessing challenges: Handling overfitting when training ML models on network traffic data. Dataset used: CES-CICIDS2018 — a benchmark dataset for evaluating intrusion detection systems. Session 5 – From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin (Speaker: Anh Vinh) Anh Vinh shared his career journey and practical interview advice:
Career Path Started as IT Helpdesk → grew into System Administrator (responsible for OS management and 24/7 system uptime). Real incident: Experienced a system crash caused by telesale users overwhelming the system. Hardware failure (disk crash) required quick action — shut down non-essential services and migrated data to a cloud server so the business could continue operating. This experience triggered his transition to Cloud/DevOps Engineer. Interview Preparation Tips Study knowledge following a structured roadmap Prepare a polished CV Research the position and company on LinkedIn before the interview Interview Reality: 30% Technical, 70% Systems Thinking Example question: “If an e-commerce platform reports a feature bug, how do you troubleshoot?” Start debugging from the user side → trace to the service/backend → describe findings to managers → check logs → if nothing found, investigate the database. Example question: “If you have an e-commerce product page, how do you deploy it to production? What infrastructure does the system need?” Key Advice Build end-to-end projects to develop systems thinking and incident response skills. Interviewers value your problem-solving mindset more than memorized technical answers. Lessons Learned & Personal Takeaways Serverless game development is viable but has trade-offs: DynamoDB scan costs and Lambda’s stateless nature require careful architecture decisions for real-time multiplayer games. Containers dramatically improve resource efficiency: Docker enables running multiple services on fewer instances, reducing infrastructure costs. GraphRAG > traditional RAG: Adding relationship awareness through graph structures produces significantly better LLM context for complex queries. Defense in depth: Combining rule-based WAF with ML-based intrusion detection creates a more robust security posture. Systems thinking over memorization: Real-world interviews and jobs require the ability to trace issues end-to-end and think holistically about infrastructure. Event Photos

title: Data & Identity
content:
The data tier (RDS), the secrets store (SSM), and the identity setup (Cognito pool + the app’s RS256 JWT keys).
Step 3 — RDS MySQL Multi-AZ RDS → Subnet groups → saashr-db-subnets = data-1a + data-1b. Create database → MySQL → db.t4g.micro → Multi-AZ: Yes (create a standby). Search Aurora and RDS -> Databases -> Click Create Database -> Choose Full Configuration 3. Storage type: gp3; Storage 20 GB gp3; Enable Storage Autoscaling: Untick. 4. VPC = saashr-vpc, subnet group = saashr-db-subnets, Public access = No, SG = sg-rds. Database create successfully After creation, run database/init.sql to create auth_db, tenant_db, hr_db (via a temporary bastion). create ec2 Cut the init.sql file into ec2 Connect ec2 to database 📎 Attachment: database/init.sql (place under 5.4-Data-Identity/files/).
Step 4 — Secrets (SSM Parameter Store) Store configuration and secrets in Systems Manager → Parameter Store as SecureString (free), later referenced by the ECS task definition — nothing is hard-coded in the images.
Search for and open Systems Manager → Parameter Store. Click Create parameter. 3. For each parameter below: enter the Name, set Type = SecureString, paste the Value, then Create parameter. Repeat for all 7. Parameter name Holds /saashr/cognito/client_id Cognito app client ID /saashr/cognito/pool_id Cognito user pool ID /saashr/db/host RDS endpoint (host) /saashr/db/password RDS database password /saashr/jwt/private_key RS256 JWT signing private key /saashr/jwt/public_key RS256 JWT verification public key /saashr/sqs/url SQS queue URL On AWS Console -> click Cloudshell at the bottom -> patse this command to get private key and public key Generating the RS256 JWT keypair /saashr/jwt/private_key and /saashr/jwt/public_key are an RSA keypair. Generate them once in AWS CloudShell, then paste the PEM output into the two SecureString parameters above:
python3 -c " from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization key = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048) priv = key.private_bytes(serialization.Encoding.PEM, serialization.PrivateFormat.TraditionalOpenSSL, serialization.NoEncryption()).decode() pub = key.public_key().public_bytes(serialization.Encoding.PEM, serialization.PublicFormat.SubjectPublicKeyInfo).decode() print('=== PRIVATE KEY ===') print(priv) print('=== PUBLIC KEY ===') print(pub) " ⚠️ Never publish the real private key. Show only this command — redact the printed key and mask the /saashr/jwt/private_key value in any screenshot. Anyone holding the private key can forge JWTs for the app.
db/password and jwt/private_key must be SecureString. The same values can be set from the CLI, e.g. aws ssm put-parameter --name "/saashr/db/host" --type SecureString --value "<rds-endpoint>".
Step 5 — Cognito Search for and open Cognito → User pools.
Click Create user pool and configure:
Application type: Traditional web application Name your application: saashr-app Sign-in identifiers: Email Self-registration: Enable self-registration Return URL: https://localhost (dev; use the CloudFront URL in production) Then click Create user directory to create the pool.
Open the new user pool → App clients.
Click Create app client:
Application type: Traditional web application Name your application: saashr-app Return URL: https://localhost Then click Create app client to finish.
Note the User pool ID and App client ID → store them in SSM as /saashr/cognito/pool_id and /saashr/cognito/client_id (Step 4).
This pool provides the user directory and login. Tenant isolation (tenant_id) and roles are carried in the application’s RS256 JWT (keys from Step 4).

content:
Week 5 Objectives: Containerization and application deployment strategies Continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) pipelines Hybrid cloud storage and centralized repositories Data lake architecture and NoSQL database modeling Cost optimization models and compute resource right-sizing Cost visualization, data cataloging, and analytical query execution Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Deploy Application on Docker & Amazon ECS: Study containerization concepts, deployment workflows, and ECS container orchestration (Clusters, Tasks, Services, Load Balancing, Auto-Scaling, and Deployment Strategies). 05/18/2026 05/18/2026 https://000015.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000016.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 - AWS CodePipeline Automation: Study continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) concepts and automation pipelines using AWS CodePipeline. 05/19/2026 05/19/2026 https://000017.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000023.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 - AWS Storage Gateway & FSx: Study hybrid cloud storage solutions using File Storage Gateway and high-performance file sharing using Amazon FSx. 05/20/2026 05/20/2026 https://000024.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000025.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 - Data Lake & DynamoDB Architecture: Study the principles of building a Data Lake on AWS and advanced database modeling using Amazon DynamoDB. 05/21/2026 05/21/2026 https://000035.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000039.awsstudygroup.com/ 6 - AWS Cost Optimization: Study AWS Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, Reserved DB Instances, and EC2 resource optimization techniques. 05/22/2026 05/22/2026 https://000042.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000032.awsstudygroup.com/ 7 - Cost Visualization & Analytics: Study AWS Cost Explorer, usage visualization, and advanced cost analysis using AWS Glue and Amazon Athena. 05/23/2026 05/23/2026 https://000034.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000040.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 5 Achievements: Containerized Deployment with Docker & AWS Services
Studied the core containerization components including Docker Hub, Nginx reverse proxy, Amazon ECR, Amazon RDS, and Amazon EC2. Explored the client-to-database workflow: traffic routing via Internet Gateway and Nginx, frontend/backend container interaction, and RDS data persistence. Researched local container deployment methodologies compared to running containers on cloud virtual servers (EC2). Studied RDS capabilities such as DB subnet groups, Multi-AZ deployments, read replicas, and automatic storage scaling. Understood application orchestration using Docker Compose and the process of pushing/pulling images from Amazon ECR and Docker Hub. Container Orchestration with Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS)
Researched Amazon ECS architecture and key components: Clusters, Task Definitions, Services, and container hosts (EC2 vs. Fargate). Studied Task Definitions as blueprints defining Docker image, CPU/memory allocations, network mode, log configurations, and execution IAM roles. Explored resource and application scaling mechanisms: Cluster Scaling (scaling instances/Fargate resources) and Service Scaling (adjusting running container tasks via CloudWatch). Studied application update strategies: Rolling Deployments (gradual container replacement) and Blue/Green Deployments (parallel environments with traffic shifting via CodeDeploy and ALB). Researched infrastructure preparations including CodeDeploy execution roles, NAT Gateways, Route Tables, and Application Load Balancers (ALB) for frontend/backend service routing. Continuous Integration, Delivery, and Monitoring (CI/CD, Container Insights, FireLens)
Studied the core concepts of Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD) to automate code building, testing, and deployment. Compared CI/CD workflows and automation pipelines across GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD, and AWS CodePipeline (integrating CodeBuild and CodeDeploy). Understood pipeline automation stages: triggering via git tags, building Docker images, pushing to ECR/Docker Hub, updating Task Definitions, and updating ECS Services (via CodeDeploy blue/green or rolling). Researched automated application deployment to Amazon EC2 instances using the AWS native developer suite (CodePipeline, CodeBuild, and CodeDeploy). Studied CodeCommit’s lifecycle status (deprecated for new customers as of July 2024) and the procedures for migrating repository data to other SCM providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) using git mirroring techniques. Understood the roles of AWS CodeBuild in compiling and testing code on dynamic build servers, and AWS CodeDeploy (leveraging CodeDeploy Agents on EC2) in automating application installation. Explored secure pipeline artifact storage on Amazon S3 with encryption at rest managed by AWS Key Management Service (KMS) keys. Researched container performance monitoring using Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights to track CPU, memory, network utilization, and task replica states. Studied log management and routing architectures using AWS FireLens to direct container logs to external targets like Amazon S3 and CloudWatch Logs. Hybrid Storage & File Sharing (AWS Storage Gateway & FSx)
Studied AWS Storage Gateway architecture, focusing on how File Storage Gateway acts as a bridge to synchronize data between on-premises environments and Amazon S3. Researched virtual deployment configurations of the Storage Gateway appliance on virtualization platforms (VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Linux KVM) or using pre-configured EC2 AMIs. Understood the process of creating and configuring File Shares (NFS/SMB) and mounting them to local or on-premises machines to enable unlimited cloud-backed storage integration. Researched Amazon FSx for Windows File Server architecture, including EC2 file servers using the SMB protocol, S3 data replication, VPC isolation, and ENI-based networking. Studied domain integration via Windows Active Directory and the setup of highly available file systems using SSD or HDD Multi-AZ configurations. Explored advanced file server administration features: data deduplication, shadow copies (VSS), user session management, storage quotas, and Continuous Access (CA) file shares. Data Lake Building & NoSQL Architecture (DynamoDB)
Studied the core concepts and properties of a Data Lake on AWS, including raw data collection, multi-user exploration, and flexible access patterns. Researched serverless data ingestion pipelines using Amazon Kinesis Firehose Delivery Streams and sample data generation via Kinesis Data Generator. Studied AWS Glue serverless ETL architecture: cataloging metadata with Glue Crawlers, managing schemas in the Glue Data Catalog, and generating Python Spark jobs for data transformation. Explored data transformation environments using SageMaker Notebooks to prepare datasets for downstream analysis. Researched ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 using Amazon Athena (ANSI SQL Presto engine) and data visualization architectures using Amazon QuickSight (Data sources, Datasets, Analyses, Visuals, and Dashboards). Researched Amazon DynamoDB features as a fully managed, single-digit millisecond key-value and document database at any scale. Studied NoSQL data modeling best practices, advanced design patterns, and architectural design tradeoffs (such as primary keys and indexes) using the AWS CLI and Console. Researched global serverless database configurations using DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-region replication and low-latency access. Studied Change Data Capture (CDC) mechanisms using DynamoDB Streams integrated with AWS Lambda to build serverless event-driven architectures. Explored modern zero-ETL integration patterns, such as continuously indexing DynamoDB catalogs into Amazon OpenSearch Service to enable natural language queries supported by Amazon Bedrock. AWS Cost Optimization Strategies
Studied AWS Savings Plans (Compute and EC2 Instance types) as flexible pricing models requiring 1- or 3-year hourly commitments with payment options (All, Partial, or No Upfront). Researched Savings Plan Recommendations, lookback periods, queuing/scheduling future plans, and sharing cost savings across AWS Organizations. Compared Standard and Convertible Reserved Instances (RIs) regarding flexibility (instance family, OS, region, AZ modifications), discounts, and standard RI reselling on the RI Marketplace. Researched Amazon RDS Reserved DB Instances, including configuration matching requirements (DB engine, instance class, Multi-AZ) to apply discounts and behaviors when instance sizes auto-scale. Researched EC2 right-sizing using Amazon CloudWatch Agent to monitor custom metrics like memory utilization alongside default metrics (CPU utilization, Network In/Out). Studied CloudWatch Agent deployment on EC2 nodes, including IAM policy configuration (CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy) and configuration wizard setups. Researched Amazon EC2 Resource Optimization (via AWS Cost Explorer) to analyze a 14-day history and recommend optimization actions: Downsizing (underutilized, 1%–40% CPU) or Terminating (idle, <=1% CPU). Understood right-sizing best practices: optimizing before migrating, measuring workloads extensively, starting with non-production environments, and managing burstable instance families (T-types) separately. Cost Visualization & Analytics (AWS Glue & Athena)
Researched cost monitoring and visualization tools including AWS Cost Explorer and AWS Budgets for threshold alerts. Studied data cataloging using AWS Glue Crawlers to catalog Cost and Usage Reports (CUR) stored in Amazon S3. Researched ad-hoc SQL query analysis using Amazon Athena to query and analyze cataloged cost datasets.

title: Async Messaging (SQS)
content:
tenant-service publishes business events; hr-service consumes them asynchronously. The queue decouples the two and absorbs spikes; a Dead-Letter Queue catches poison messages.
Step 6 — SQS + DLQ Search for and open Simple Queue Service → Queues. Create the main queue saashr-events: Type: Standard Name: saashr-events Keep the default configuration, but set Receive message wait time = 20 (enables long-polling — matches the consumer). Access policy: Advanced Dead-letter queue → “Set this queue to receive undeliverable messages” = Enabled Choose queue: arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:<account-id>:saashr-events-dlq Maximum receives: 5 Click Create queue. 3. Create the dead-letter queue saashr-events-dlq:
Type: Standard Name: saashr-events-dlq Access policy: Advanced Click Create queue. Note the saashr-events Queue URL → save into SSM as /saashr/sqs/url (Step 4). The console only lets you select a DLQ that already exists. If so, create saashr-events-dlq first, then enable the redrive policy on saashr-events.
Application code (already implemented) Publisher — tenant-service/app/core/sqs.py (boto3, uses the ECS task-role credentials, no static keys):
# send_message(QueueUrl=SQS_QUEUE_URL, MessageBody=json.dumps(event)) Consumer — hr-service/app/core/worker.py (start_sqs_consumer): long-poll → process → delete_message on success (failures retry, then land in the DLQ).
📎 Attachment: sqs.py, worker.py (place under 5.5-Async-SQS/files/).
Event-driven decoupling via SQS is the scalability story for the async path — see also Security & IAM for the task-role SQS permissions.

title: Workshop
content:
SaaS HR Multi-Tenant on AWS Overview A multi-tenant HR SaaS (FastAPI microservices + React) deployed on AWS as a 3-tier architecture: CloudFront + S3 for the frontend, ECS Fargate behind an Application Load Balancer for the API, and RDS MySQL Multi-AZ for data. Async events use Amazon SQS, identity uses Amazon Cognito, monitoring uses CloudWatch + SNS. Built by hand in the AWS Console in ap-southeast-1.
Each customer company is an isolated tenant — data is separated per tenant.
This workshop uses more than 3 AWS services (Cognito, SQS, RDS, ECS Fargate, ALB, S3, CloudFront, CloudWatch, SNS, SSM) and covers architecture design, end-to-end deployment, testing, and clean-up.
Content Overview & Architecture Prerequisite Networking Data & Identity Async Messaging (SQS) Application Tier (ECS) Frontend & CDN Monitoring & Alerts Testing & Validation Security & IAM Clean-up

title: Application Tier (ECS)
content:
Push the images to ECR, put an ALB in front, then run the 3 services on ECS Fargate with auto scaling.
Step 8 — ECR + push images Search ECR → choose Private registry -> Repositories -> Create Repository: create 3 repos: saashr-auth, saashr-tenant, saashr-hr. 3 repositories created successfully. 2. Build and push each (example for auth):
aws ecr get-login-password --region ap-southeast-1 \ | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com docker build -t saashr-auth ./microservices/auth-service docker tag saashr-auth:latest <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/saashr-auth:latest docker push <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/saashr-auth:latest Login aws cli Push from cli (hr’s push): 3 ECR repos with a pushed image tag 📎 Attachment: the three Dockerfiles (place under 5.6-Compute-ECS/files/).
Step 9 — Application Load Balancer Target groups (type IP, one per service): tg-auth(port 8000), tg-tenant (port 8001), tg-hr(port 8002); Health check /api/v1/{service}/health. Healthy thresold = 2 (60s -> deploying faster) Continue with tg-tenant and tg-hr Search EC2 → Load Balancers → Create ALB saashr-alb, internet-facing, subnets = public-1a + public-1b, SG = sg-alb. Select Application Load balancer -> create Name: saashr-alb Choose: internet facing ipv4 VPC: saashr-vpc subnets = public-1a + public-1b Listeners and routing: Choose Forward to target groups protocol: HTTP Port: 80 Target group: tg-auth -> Create ALB created successfully Listener :80 rules → /api/v1/auth/*→tg-auth, /api/v1/tenants/*→tg-tenant, /api/v1/hr/*→tg-hr. Then click to HTTP:80 Click Add Rule Conditions -> Path: /api/v1/auth/* Target group: tg-auth Continue with 3 rules left. Step 10 — ECS Fargate + Auto Scaling ECS → Create cluster saashr-cluster (Fargate). Task definitions (one per service, Fargate, 0.25 vCPU / 0.5 GB):
container image = ECR repo, port = app port.
Task role (least privilege): sqs:SendMessage/ReceiveMessage/DeleteMessage (tenant/hr), cognito-idp:* minimal (auth), ssm:GetParameters.
Execution role: ECR pull + CloudWatch Logs + SSM read.
Secrets from SSM; log driver awslogs → /ecs/saashr/{service}. 1.1. Create task definition for auth:
Name: saashr-auth
CPU: .25 vCPU
Memory: .5 GB task role: None
task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole
Container name: saashr-auth
Image URI: copy from auth repository in ECR repositories
port: 8000
Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: 1.2. Create task definition for tenant: name: saashr-tenant CPU: .25 vCPU Memory: .5 GB task role: create new role Role name: saashr-tenant-task-role Policy -> Create new policy Replace s3:GetObject with sqs:SendMessage and “arn:aws:s3:::[[readS3Paths]]/*” with "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:016461465939:saashr-events" Click Create role task role: saashr-tenant-task-role task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole Container name: saashr-auth Image URI: copy from tenant repository in ECR repositories port: 8001 Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: 1.3. Create task definition for hr: name: saashr-hr CPU: .25 vCPU Memory: .5 GB task role: create new role Role name: saashr-tenant-task-role Policy -> Create new policy Replace “s3:GetObject” with "sqs:ReceiveMessage","sqs:DeleteMessage","sqs:GetQueueAttributes" and “arn:aws:s3:::[[readS3Paths]]/*” with "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:016461465939:saashr-events" Click Create role task role: saashr-hr-task-role task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole Container name: saashr-auth Image URI: copy from tenant repository in ECR repositories port: 8002 Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: Services (one per task def): launch type Fargate, subnets = app-1a + app-1b, SG = sg-ecs, assign public IP = DISABLED, desired count 2, attach to the matching target group. title: Monitoring & Alerts
content:
Goal: monitor the ECS services — when a service’s CPU Utilization reaches ≥ 70%, CloudWatch triggers an alarm and emails the team through Amazon SNS.
Step 7 — Amazon SNS (topic + email subscription) 7.1 Create the SNS topic Amazon SNS → Topics → Create topic.
Type: Standard Name: saashr-alerts Display name: saashr-alerts Click Create topic. 7.2 Create the email subscription Open the saashr-alerts topic → Subscriptions tab → Create subscription.
Protocol: Email Endpoint: <team-email> Click Create subscription. 7.3 Confirm the email Amazon SNS sends a confirmation email to that address. Open it and click Confirm subscription. The status changes from Pending confirmation to Confirmed.
Step 13 — CloudWatch Alarm → SNS 13.1 Create alarm → select metric CloudWatch → Alarms → Create alarm → Select metric.
13.2 Choose the ECS metric In Select metric, follow ECS → ClusterName, ServiceName:
Cluster: saashr-cluster2 Service: saashr-hr-svc Metric: CPUUtilization Click Select metric. 13.3 Configure the condition Statistic: Average Period: 1 minute Threshold type: Static Whenever CPUUtilization is: Greater/Equal Threshold value: 70 The alarm enters the ALARM state when CPU Utilization is ≥ 70% over a 1-minute period.
13.4 Configure the action Alarm state trigger: In alarm Notification → Select an existing SNS topic → saashr-alerts CloudWatch emails every confirmed subscriber of the topic when the alarm fires.
13.5 Name the alarm Alarm name: saashr-alerts-alarm-noti (add a description if needed) Click Next → Create alarm. 13.6 Verify The new alarm appears with:
Metric: CPUUtilization · Threshold: ≥ 70% · Action: SNS Notification State: OK (or Insufficient data at first) Open it to watch the CPU Utilization graph, the threshold line, and the alarm history.
This alarm watches one service (saashr-hr-svc). Repeat 13.1–13.5 for auth and tenant to cover all three.
Logs Each service also streams logs to the CloudWatch Logs group /ecs/saashr/{service} (configured in the ECS task definition, Step 10).

title: Week 9 Worklog
content:
Week 9 Objectives: Consolidate and synchronize all 4 architecture design documents (EN + VI) and the official PNG diagram. Finalize and unify the request flows (traffic flows) across the whole system. Update the architecture diagram so it matches the documentation 100%. Re-estimate the monthly cost and submit the finalized design to the admin team for review. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 6 - Remove all inconsistencies in infrastructure, data flow and technical specs 19/06/2026 19/06/2026 Internal design docs 7 - Define and unify the 9 request flows (DNS, Static Frontend, API, ALB routing, Redis Pub/Sub, SQL, Cognito, Secrets, SIEM logs) - Detail ECR / CloudWatch Logs / RDS backup parameters - Reposition Wazuh SOC as a “demo SIEM” and confirm the NAT-less network design 20/06/2026 20/06/2026 Internal design docs 8 - Update aws_architecture_diagram.png to match the documentation 100% (Internet Gateway / NAT-less, 9 traffic flows, 3 logical RDS databases) - Re-estimate the monthly cost (≈ $70–$95 / month) 21/06/2026 21/06/2026 https://calculator.aws/ 2 - Finalize the architecture package (4 docs + 9 flows + PNG diagram) - Send the finalized architecture to the admin team for review / reference - Receive admin feedback: the whole architecture and all 9 flows were rejected - Deleted the entire architecture and all flows, and started redrawing everything from scratch the following week 22/06/2026 22/06/2026 Week 9 Achievements: Synchronized all 4 design documents (English + Vietnamese) so there were no remaining conflicts in infrastructure, data flow, or technical specifications.
Finalized the 9 unified request flows:
Flow 1 – DNS access (Route 53 → CloudFront) Flow 2 – Static Frontend (CloudFront → S3 via OAC) Flow 3 – API traffic (CloudFront → ALB, HTTPS 443) Flow 4 – ALB path-based routing (/auth, /tenants, /hr → ECS tasks) Flow 5 – Redis Pub/Sub (Tenant/HR ↔ Redis Broker, port 6379) Flow 6 – SQL Database (services → RDS MySQL, port 3306) Flow 7 – Cognito authentication (Frontend & Auth Service → Cognito, JWT) Flow 8 – Secrets retrieval (services → SSM Parameter Store) Flow 9 – SIEM log streaming (ECS tasks → Wazuh Manager, port 1514/1515) Updated diagram (Internet Gateway / NAT-less design, 9 traffic flows, 3 logical databases in RDS MySQL). Re-estimated the monthly cost at ≈ $70–$95 / month (RDS and Wazuh running 24/7), within the $120–$150 ceiling budget with an AWS Budgets hard alert at $100.
Outcome: After the finalized architecture and flows were sent to the admin team for review, the design was rejected. The entire architecture and all flows were removed, and a complete redesign was started from scratch in the following week — which later became the corrected 3-tier architecture.

title: Demo, Testing & Validation
content:
End-to-end validation of the deployed stack via the CloudFront entry point, followed by error testing, high-availability testing, and observability. Base URL:
BASE="https://d3rpqs3arfqpga.cloudfront.net/" UI Demo videos 🎥 Video link: UI walkthrough — 2 tenants — two tabs, one account per tenant, covering dashboard, employee list, department list, attendance check-in, leave request submission, tenant members, and profile. 🎥 Video link: Employee & department management — create/edit/delete employees and edit departments. 🎥 Video link: Attendance & leave approval — check-in/check-out flow and leave request submission with admin approval. 1. Health checks (smoke test) curl -s $BASE/api/v1/auth/health curl -s $BASE/api/v1/tenants/health curl -s $BASE/api/v1/hr/health Result — all three healthy (the database:up fields confirm ECS and RDS are reachable):
{"status":"healthy","service":"auth-service"} {"status":"healthy","database":"up","service":"tenant-service"} {"status":"healthy","database":"up","service":"hr-service"} 2. Frontend delivery curl -I $BASE/ Result: HTTP/2 200, content-type: text/html — the React SPA is served through CloudFront (from the private S3 origin).
3. Authentication — end-to-end # Register a workspace + owner curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/auth/register -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"email":"owner@acmedemo.com","password":"Passw0rd!","tenant_name":"Acme Demo","subdomain":"acmedemo"}' # Log in → receive an access token curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/auth/login -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"email":"owner@acmedemo.com","password":"Passw0rd!","subdomain":"acmedemo"}' # Verify the token curl -s $BASE/api/v1/auth/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" Result — /me returns the authenticated identity with the correct role:
{"id":"usr_9476d71e-...","email":"owner@acmedemo.com","status":"active","active_tenant_id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","role":"owner"} The token is an app-signed RS256 JWT (auth-service signs with the private key; the other services verify with the public key — see Data & Identity). Its claims (tenant_id, role) drive every downstream authorization check.
4. Business operations (authenticated, tenant-scoped) curl -s $BASE/api/v1/tenants/my-tenant -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/hr/departments -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"name":"Engineering"}' curl -s $BASE/api/v1/hr/employees -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" Result:
{"id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","name":"Acme Demo","subdomain":"acmedemo","status":"active","plan_tier":"basic","created_at":"2026-07-08T00:24:15"} {"id":"dept_bcd98e2a-...","tenant_id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","name":"Engineering","description":null,"created_at":"2026-07-08T00:27:25"} [] Reading the tenant, creating a department (201), and listing employees (empty for a new tenant) all succeed — data reads/writes to RDS work through the authenticated path.
5. Validation & error testing API error responses are tested directly against the ALB (http://<alb-dns>/...). CloudFront’s SPA custom error responses map 403/404 to index.html (200), which masks API error codes for browser clients — so the API tier is validated at the ALB, where server: uvicorn confirms the request reached the application.
Test Command Observed result No token curl -i $ALB/api/v1/hr/employees 403 Forbidden {"detail":"Not authenticated"} ✅ Wrong password login with a bad password 401 Unauthorized {"detail":"Invalid email or password"} ✅ Duplicate subdomain register an existing subdomain 409 {"detail":"Tenant subdomain already registered."} ✅ Duplicate department create a department with an existing name Blocked by the DB unique constraint uq_tenant_dept (see note) Tenant isolation register tenant B, log in as B, GET /api/v1/hr/employees Returns only B’s data — A’s records are never visible Observed — duplicate subdomain is rejected:
{"detail":"Tenant subdomain already registered."} Known limitation: a duplicate department name is correctly prevented at the database level (unique key uq_tenant_dept), but the API currently surfaces it as HTTP 500 instead of a clean 409 Conflict. The data integrity is safe; only the error response needs improving.
6. Async messaging (SQS) — testing finding End-to-end testing of the asynchronous path (tenant status change → SQS → hr-service) surfaced an integration issue in the current deployment, which is exactly what this stage of testing is meant to catch:
Infrastructure is healthy — a message sent manually to saashr-events was accepted (the NumberOfMessagesSent / SentMessageSize metrics increased), confirming the queue and DLQ are correctly provisioned. Producer — tenant-service did not publish to the queue on a status change (no MessagesSent from the app), despite the task role holding sqs:SendMessage. Consumer — the queued test message was not drained (MessagesVisible = 1, MessagesDeleted = 0), so the consumer was not processing during the test window. Assessment: the event-driven design (SQS decoupling + DLQ + long-polling consumer, see Async Messaging) is sound; the gap is in the deployment wiring (likely a stale service image and/or consumer startup), and the fix is in progress. This is recorded here as an honest testing finding rather than claimed as passing.
7. Observability Logs: CloudWatch Logs groups /ecs/saashr-auth, /ecs/saashr-tenant, /ecs/saashr-hr capture request logs and unhandled errors. Metrics: CloudWatch → ECS CPUUtilization per service. Alert → email: trigger the alarm without generating real load: aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state --alarm-name saashr-alerts-alarm-noti \ --state-value ALARM --state-reason "manual test" Expected: the SNS email arrives within seconds and the alarm shows the ALARM state (see Monitoring & Alerts).

title: Security & IAM
content:
How the design applies least privilege, keeps resources private, and avoids hard-coded credentials.
Principle of Least Privilege Each ECS service assumes its own task role with only the actions it needs:
Service Allowed actions tenant sqs:SendMessage on saashr-events hr sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage on saashr-events auth minimal cognito-idp (InitiateAuth), ssm:GetParameters all ssm:GetParameters (own params), CloudWatch Logs write Example — the tenant task role (scoped to one queue, one action):
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [{ "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sqs:SendMessage", "Resource": "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:<acct>:saashr-events" }] } The task role with its inline least-privilege policy → No hard-coded credentials boto3 uses the ECS task-role credentials at runtime — no static access keys in code or images. Secrets (DB password, SQS URL, Cognito IDs) come from SSM Parameter Store at task start, not from the repo. Private by default ECS and RDS run in private subnets with no public IP. S3 blocks all public access; served only via CloudFront OAC. RDS data subnets have no route to the internet. Zero-trust security groups sg-alb → sg-ecs → sg-rds are chained so each tier only accepts traffic from the tier in front (see Networking). The database is unreachable from the internet.
📷 [Screenshot] S3 “Block all public access = On” + RDS “Publicly accessible = No” → /images/5-Workshop/5.10-Security-IAM/private-resources.png

title: Clean-up
content:
Delete resources in reverse dependency order to avoid ongoing charges. The NAT Gateway, RDS, and ALB bill by the hour — remove these first if you only want to pause.
Teardown order CloudFront — disable, then delete the distribution. S3 — empty, then delete saashr-frontend-<acct>. ALB — delete the load balancer + the 3 target groups. ECS — set services to desired 0, delete the services, then the cluster. NAT Gateway — delete (stops the biggest hourly charge). RDS — delete the database instance (skip or take a final snapshot). VPC endpoints (if any) — delete. Elastic IP — release the NAT’s EIP. VPC — delete (removes subnets, route tables, IGW, security groups). Then remove the standalone services: 10. SNS topic + CloudWatch alarms + log groups. 11. ECR repositories (delete images first). 12. Cognito user pool, SSM parameters, ACM cert (if a custom domain was used).
Double-check the Billing → Budgets page a day later to confirm charges have stopped. Leftover NAT Gateways and RDS instances are the usual surprise bills.
